## prompt-1-mermaid

*Generated by Process A from the submitted `prompts/prompt-adoption.md`
(SUB-PROMPT SCHEDULE item 1), adapted from the prior build's
`review/sub-prompts/prompt-1-mermaid.md` and the curated colored Mermaid catalog
in `review/mermaid/`. This build does not rewrite the prior review; it produces an
analogous, independent set for the clinician confidence framework.*

Produce the curated colored Mermaid set for the clinician adoption framework into
`law-physical-ai-trials/adoption/mermaid/`. Deliver **20 high-quality,
professional Mermaid figures** (the schedule requires 20+), each in its own file
under `mermaid/diagrams/` and each pushed as its own commit, plus a catalog
`output-mermaid.md` that embeds all 20 for GitHub rendering and a directory
`README.md` naming the upstream files used.

**Palette (strict).** Use only black, grayscales, and the three theme colors
`#EBCB8B` (gold, confidence and benefit), `#D08770` (clay, harm and risk), and
`#8B2E3F` (burgundy, verification, the gate, and clinician authority). This keeps
the existing `#8B2E3F` paper template theme. Do not introduce any other hue. Fills
are the theme colors or pale gray tints; strokes are black or the deep theme
color; text is black on light fills and white on the `#8B2E3F` fill. No neon, no
gradients, no cartoon rounded cards (corner radius 2 to 4 pixels only).

**Style discipline (from `review/mermaid`).** Crisp near-rectangular boxes;
fluent spline connectors (`basis`, `natural`, `cardinal`, `catmullRom`,
`monotoneX`, `monotoneY`); named `classDef` node classes and container subgraphs
so each diagram scales to a more complex workflow; black text on a white page
background. Use a different and most relevant Mermaid diagram type for each figure
so no two figures repeat the same pattern unnecessarily (flowchart, sequence,
state, class, journey, timeline, quadrant, mindmap, gitGraph, block, sankey, C4,
requirement, and high-complexity maps).

**Subject re-theming.** Re-theme every diagram to the framework's subject so the
set can illustrate the eight confidence-question sections and the bedside
adoption argument:

1. The bedside trust decision (a clinician confronts a recommendation, runs the
   eight questions, then relies, escalates, or withholds).
2. The eight confidence questions as a clinician's reasoning path from competence
   to workflow.
3. Verification before generation, the clinician's view (Claude Code proposes,
   Codex reviews, ten gates, ACCEPT / ESCALATE / BLOCK).
4. The ten VVUQ gates seen from the clinician's seat, ending in a catastrophe
   predicate that hard-blocks.
5. The competence evidence stack (bench to prospective validation to the assurance
   run) and the calibrated-trust band between over-trust and under-trust.
6. Safety containment, the audit trail a clinician can show, and the
   human-in-the-loop oversight loop.
7. Subgroup equity reporting, reliability and drift over time, and uncertainty
   quantification at the decision.
8. Accountability roles, the escalation pathway, tumor-board defensibility, the
   night-shift scenario, the override and stop authority, the adoption maturity
   ladder, the system context, and the capstone from bedside confidence to
   trial-wide trust.

For every figure, give a one-line title, the Mermaid diagram type, the framework
slot it serves (which of the eight questions, the introduction, the framework, or
an appendix), and a one-line note on why that diagram type is correct for that
content. State that each Markdown Mermaid figure is reproduced in the compiled
LaTeX framework as a matching **colored TikZ** figure in the same palette, and
that these figures are refined across the draft, full, and final stages.

One commit per diagram file (20 commits), then one commit for `output-mermaid.md`
and one for `README.md`. For the second-to-last commit fix all errors across all
files; for the last commit write the directory README (repository structure,
badges, a representative colored Mermaid diagram, table of contents) and refresh
the pull request description. Commit and push in real time; do not hold commits.
Do not stall, ask questions, or go into plan mode.
